title: Senecio gregorii
intro: Senecio gregorii, also known as woolly-headed ragwort, is a perennial herb native to Western Australia. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, coughs, and respiratory issues, due to its anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Senecio gregorii include sesquiterpene lactones, such as senecionine and seneciphylline, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. In herbal preparations, Senecio gregorii is commonly used in teas, infusions, and tinctures to treat respiratory conditions and promote cough relief, although its use should be cautious and under the guidance of a healthcare professional due to potential toxicity.
taxonomy: Senecio gregorii is a species of flowering plant in the daisy family (Asteraceae). It is native to Western Australia and is also known as the Gregor's ragwort. As a member of the Senecio genus, it is related to other daisy-like plants found in various parts of the world. The classification of Senecio gregorii is further refined under the kingdom Plantae, phylum Angiosperms, and class Magnoliopsida. This classification reflects its botanical characteristics and evolutionary history.

side_effects: Senecio gregorii is a toxic plant due to its high concentration of pyrrolizidine alkaloids (PAs). If ingested, these alkaloids can cause severe liver damage and may lead to cirrhosis or even liver failure. Ingestion of the plant may also cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Prolonged exposure to the plant's toxic compounds may lead to more severe health issues. It is essential to avoid ingesting any parts of the Senecio gregorii plant to prevent potential harm.
